content: MG diagnosis requires clinical features plus confirmatory testing. Bedside tests: ice pack test (ptosis improvement after 2 minutes of ice application—sensitive for ocular MG), sustained upgaze test (induces/worsens ptosis). Serology: AChR antibodies (binding, blocking, modulating) positive in 85% generalized, 50% ocular MG; MuSK antibodies in AChR-negative patients (more bulbar/respiratory involvement); LRP4, agrin antibodies in double-seronegative cases. Electrodiagnostics: repetitive nerve stimulation (RNS) shows >10% decrement at 2-3Hz; single-fiber EMG (SFEMG) shows increased jitter—most sensitive test (>95%) but non-specific. Chest CT/MRI to evaluate thymus (thymoma vs hyperplasia). Differential diagnosis: Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome (LEMS—proximal weakness improves with use, associated with SCLC, anti-VGCC antibodies, incremental RNS response), botulism (descending paralysis, pupillary involvement), congenital myasthenic syndromes (genetic, childhood onset), mitochondrial myopathies, oculopharyngeal muscular dystrophy. MGFA clinical classification grades severity (Class I: ocular only; Class II-IV: varying generalized weakness; Class V: intubation). QMG score quantifies severity for monitoring.
